h1Title: Acute Vaginitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute vaginitis is sudden-onset inflammation of the vaginal mucosa that produces discharge, itching, burning, and odor, typically from infection by bacteria, yeast, or protozoa. Bacterial vaginosis accounts for roughly 50% of all vaginal infections, vulvovaginal candidiasis for 25-30%, and trichomoniasis for 5-15% in reproductive-age women worldwide (CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021). Symptoms onset within days and can disrupt quality of life, sexual function, and, in pregnancy, raise the risk of preterm birth and pelvic inflammatory disease. Accurate diagnosis requires microscopy or molecular testing to identify the pathogen, because symptoms overlap across subtypes. Effective treatments are available for every common cause, with cure rates exceeding 80% for first-line regimens.

keyStats.prevalence: Vaginitis accounts for over 10 million outpatient visits per year in the United States (CDC 2021); up to 75% of women experience at least one episode of vulvovaginal candidiasis in their lifetime
keyStats.demographics: Primarily affects reproductive-age women aged 15-44; trichomoniasis disproportionately affects Black women (11.1% prevalence vs 1.5% in White women, NHANES 2013-2016)
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence ages 20-35; candidal vaginitis peaks in the third decade; bacterial vaginosis peaks ages 15-44
keyStats.globalCases: Trichomoniasis alone affects an estimated 156 million people worldwide annually (WHO 2020); bacterial vaginosis affects roughly 21 million US women aged 14-49 (NHANES 2001-2004)

definition: Acute vaginitis (ICD-10: N76.0) is acute inflammation of the vaginal epithelium causing sudden discharge, vulvar pruritus, burning, and dyspareunia, typically lasting under four weeks. The condition spans several distinct etiologic entities managed under the same clinical umbrella. Bacterial vaginosis results from a shift in vaginal flora — a decline in hydrogen-peroxide-producing Lactobacillus species and overgrowth of anaerobes including Gardnerella vaginalis, Mobiluncus species, Prevotella, and Mycoplasma hominis. Vulvovaginal candidiasis is caused by Candida albicans in 80-92% of cases, with non-albicans species (C. glabrata, C. tropicalis) accounting for the remainder. Trichomonal vaginitis is a sexually transmitted protozoal infection by Trichomonas vaginalis. Non-infectious causes include contact dermatitis from spermicides, condoms, soaps, or lubricants, and atrophic or hormonal vaginitis in peri- or postmenopausal women, though the latter is classified separately. The vaginal epithelium and its microbiome are managed by gynecologists, family physicians, and sexual health clinicians. Acute vaginitis that ascends to the uterus and fallopian tubes becomes pelvic inflammatory disease, a far more dangerous complication.

typesClassification.description: Polymicrobial overgrowth of anaerobes replacing normal Lactobacillus flora. Produces a thin, gray-white, malodorous discharge with a fishy amine odor on whiff test. Not strictly an STI but associated with new or multiple sexual partners. Diagnosed by Amsel's clinical criteria or Nugent/Ison-Hay scoring on Gram stain.

Yeast overgrowth, predominantly Candida albicans, triggered by antibiotic use, high-estrogen states, immunosuppression, or uncontrolled diabetes. Produces thick, white, cottage-cheese-like discharge with intense vulvar pruritus and erythema, without significant odor. Diagnosed by wet-prep or culture showing budding yeast and pseudohyphae.

Sexually transmitted infection by the flagellated protozoon Trichomonas vaginalis. Discharge is copious, yellow-green, frothy, and malodorous; the classic 'strawberry cervix' (punctate hemorrhages) appears in roughly 2% of cases visually and 45% under colposcopy. Diagnosed by wet mount, NAAT, or point-of-care antigen test.

Hypersensitivity or irritant reaction to topical products — soaps, lubricants, spermicides, latex, fragranced products. No infectious pathogen on microscopy. Treatment is removing the offending agent; topical low-potency corticosteroid for severe inflammation.

primarySymptoms: Abnormal vaginal discharge — thin gray (BV), thick white cottage-cheese texture (candida), or frothy yellow-green (trichomoniasis) — each with distinct appearance that guides empirical treatment while awaiting tests.
Vulvar pruritus and burning, most intense in candidal vaginitis, often worsening at night and after urination.
Vaginal odor described as 'fishy' or amine-like, characteristic of bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis; absent in uncomplicated candidal vaginitis.
Dyspareunia (pain during sexual intercourse), reflecting mucosal inflammation and reduced lubrication in all subtypes.
External dysuria — burning with urination as urine contacts inflamed vulvar tissue — distinct from the internal dysuria of a urinary tract infection.
Vaginal erythema and edema visible on speculum examination, most pronounced in candidal and trichomonal infections.
Spotting or contact bleeding from friable inflamed mucosa, particularly in trichomonal vaginitis with a 'strawberry cervix' pattern.
Pelvic discomfort or mild lower abdominal heaviness, typically without adnexal tenderness on examination unless ascending infection is present.
Post-coital odor, especially with bacterial vaginosis, due to amines released when semen elevates vaginal pH above 4.5.
earlyWarningSigns: Change in normal vaginal discharge volume, color, or consistency compared to personal baseline, appearing within 24-72 hours of a trigger (new antibiotic course, new sexual partner, hormonal change).
Mild vulvar itch after antibiotic use for another infection — a prodrome of antibiotic-associated candidal overgrowth before full discharge and erythema develop.
Fishy vaginal odor after menstruation or unprotected intercourse, indicating a pH shift and early BV dysbiosis.
Unexplained urinary frequency or discomfort without a positive urine culture — early vaginitis can cause external urinary symptoms that mimic UTI.
emergencySigns: Lower abdominal pain with uterine or adnexal tenderness on bimanual examination — these signs indicate possible ascending pelvic inflammatory disease requiring immediate antibiotic therapy.
Fever above 38 °C with pelvic pain and cervical motion tenderness — suggests PID with potential tubo-ovarian abscess; refer to emergency gynecology.
Bloody or purulent discharge in a pregnant woman with contractions or cervical dilation — risk of ascending infection threatening preterm labor; urgent obstetric evaluation required.
Rapidly spreading vulvar erythema, necrotic tissue, or subcutaneous crepitus — rare but may indicate necrotizing fasciitis (Fournier's equivalent), a surgical emergency.

causes.description: Replacement of protective Lactobacillus crispatus or L. jensenii by anaerobic bacteria including Gardnerella vaginalis, Prevotella bivia, Mobiluncus species, and Mycoplasma hominis. The resulting pH rise above 4.5 further suppresses Lactobacillus, creating a self-reinforcing dysbiotic state. BV is associated with but not caused solely by sexual activity.

Candida albicans, a commensal fungus in up to 20% of asymptomatic women, transitions to a pathogenic hyphal form when host defenses are disrupted — typically by broad-spectrum antibiotics (which reduce Lactobacillus competition), high-dose estrogen (pregnancy, OCP), immunosuppression, or poorly controlled diabetes mellitus.

The flagellated protozoon is transmitted sexually, almost exclusively through penile-vaginal contact. It thrives at vaginal pH above 5.0 and attaches directly to vaginal epithelial cells, triggering neutrophil infiltration. Concurrent BV and trichomoniasis are present in up to 30% of infected women.

Systemic antibiotics — particularly broad-spectrum agents such as amoxicillin-clavulanate, clindamycin, and fluoroquinolones — reduce Lactobacillus populations by 50-80% within 48-72 hours, increasing candidal colonization rates two- to four-fold and facilitating BV dysbiosis.

causes.cause: Hormonal changes altering vaginal pH and mucosa
causes.description: Estrogen maintains vaginal glycogen that feeds Lactobacillus. Pregnancy elevates estrogen, increasing candidal risk. Menstrual-cycle fluctuations raise pH during menstruation. Peri- and postmenopausal estrogen decline causes epithelial atrophy, raising pH and susceptibility to all infectious causes.

Soaps, lubricants, scented hygiene products, spermicides containing nonoxynol-9, and latex in some condoms disrupt the vaginal epithelial barrier and local immune defense, producing a non-infectious inflammatory vaginitis that clinically resembles candidal infection.

riskFactors.description: Any broad-spectrum antibiotic course raises candidal vaginitis risk two- to four-fold and is the single most common precipitant. Risk is highest with clindamycin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, and fluoroquinolones used for ≥7 days.
BV incidence increases with new partners regardless of condom use. Trichomoniasis requires direct sexual transmission. Women with BV are 1.5-2× more likely to acquire a new STI.
Elevated vaginal glucose supports Candida hyphal growth. Women with HbA1c above 8% have a roughly 3-fold higher risk of recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis.
Elevated estrogen and progesterone increase vaginal glycogen. BV affects 10-30% of pregnant women; untreated BV in pregnancy is associated with preterm birth (OR 1.7-2.2 across meta-analyses).
Women with CD4 count below 200 cells/µL have roughly 3-fold higher rates of recurrent candidal vaginitis. Systemic corticosteroid courses lasting >2 weeks also suppress vaginal immune surveillance.

IUD use is associated with an approximately 1.5-fold increased risk of BV compared with non-IUD users; the mechanism involves biofilm formation and altered vaginal flora. This does not apply to the hormonal IUD to the same degree.

Vaginal douching disrupts protective Lactobacillus and raises vaginal pH. Women who douche regularly have a 1.8-fold increased risk of BV and 1.5-fold increased risk of pelvic inflammatory disease compared with non-douchers.

affectedDemographics: Reproductive-age women aged 15-44 account for the vast majority of cases; BV affects roughly 29% of US women in this age group (NHANES data).

Black women have significantly higher BV prevalence (51%) than Hispanic (32%) or White women (23%), a disparity linked to social determinants rather than purely biological factors (Allsworth & Peipert, Am J Obstet Gynecol 2007).
Pregnant women experience higher rates of candidal vaginitis and BV due to hormonal shifts; BV affects 10-30% of pregnancies worldwide.
Adolescent girls, particularly those with early sexual debut, have higher incidence of both trichomoniasis and BV; limited access to screening increases under-diagnosis.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with clinical history: discharge characteristics, odor, pruritus severity, recent antibiotic use, sexual history, and contraceptive method. Speculum examination evaluates discharge appearance and the vaginal and cervical mucosa. Point-of-care pH measurement with nitrazine paper is the simplest first step: pH ≤4.5 suggests candidal vaginitis; pH above 4.5 points to BV or trichomoniasis. Wet-mount microscopy remains the reference standard: clue cells (epithelial cells studded with bacteria) and positive whiff test define BV by Amsel criteria (3 of 4 criteria required: clue cells, pH >4.5, homogeneous discharge, positive amine whiff test with 10% KOH). KOH preparation for pseudohyphae and budding yeast confirms candidal vaginitis. Saline wet mount showing motile trichomonads confirms trichomoniasis, though sensitivity is only 50-70%; nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) for T. vaginalis on a vaginal swab or urine sample is the gold standard, with sensitivity >95%. Vaginal culture for Candida species is reserved for recurrent cases or treatment failure to identify non-albicans species requiring azole-resistant therapy. Gram stain with Nugent scoring (0-10 scale) is the research and reference-lab gold standard for BV. The CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines recommend vaginal NAAT panels for women with persistent or recurrent vaginitis to capture co-infections and non-albicans Candida.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Discriminates acidic (candidal; pH ≤4.5) from alkaline (BV/trichomoniasis; pH >4.5) causes at the bedside
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nitrazine strip is applied to the vaginal discharge on the speculum blade. Color change is read immediately. Takes under 30 seconds; no discomfort beyond the routine speculum examination.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies clue cells (BV), budding yeast and pseudohyphae (candida), or motile trichomonads
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab of vaginal discharge is mixed with saline on one slide and 10% potassium hydroxide on another. The lab technician examines both under a microscope; results available within 20-30 minutes at most gynecology clinics.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the amine odor released by BV anaerobes when potassium hydroxide is added to discharge
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A few drops of KOH are added to discharge on the slide; a fishy smell within seconds indicates a positive result. No additional sample is needed beyond the wet-mount swab.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects T. vaginalis DNA with >95% sensitivity and specificity, far superior to wet-mount microscopy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A vaginal swab (self-collected or clinician-collected) or first-catch urine sample is sent to the lab. Results return in 24-72 hours at most centers. The gold-standard test for trichomoniasis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies non-albicans Candida species in recurrent or treatment-refractory cases, guiding species-specific antifungal therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab of discharge is cultured on fungal agar for 48-72 hours. Results confirm species identity and can include antifungal susceptibility testing for C. glabrata.

diagnosticTests.test: Nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) panel for STIs
diagnosticTests.purpose: Simultaneously tests for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis, which can co-present with or mimic vaginitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Vaginal swab or urine sample processed with a single NAAT panel; results within 24-48 hours. Recommended by the CDC when multiple pathogens or co-infection is suspected.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is matched to the microbiological diagnosis; empirical treatment based on symptoms alone misses the pathogen in up to 50% of cases. For bacterial vaginosis, the CDC 2021 guidelines recommend oral metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 7 days, or 0.75% metronidazole vaginal gel daily for 5 days, or 2% clindamycin vaginal cream at bedtime for 7 days — all have equivalent clinical cure rates of 70-80% at 4 weeks. Clindamycin cream remains the safest for use in the first trimester of pregnancy. Lactic acid vaginal gel and boric acid 600 mg suppositories are evidence-supported adjuncts to reduce recurrence. For uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis, topical azole therapy (clotrimazole, miconazole, or terconazole vaginal cream or suppositories) achieves 80-90% cure; oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose is an equivalent and more convenient alternative per CDC guidelines. Recurrent VVC (four or more episodes per year, affecting 5-8% of reproductive-age women) requires induction therapy with fluconazole 150 mg every 72 hours for three doses, followed by weekly fluconazole 150 mg maintenance for six months, reducing recurrence by approximately 50% (Sobel et al. NEJM 2004). For trichomoniasis, oral metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 7 days produces higher cure rates than the single 2g dose in women (cure 89-95% vs 84-88%); tinidazole 2g single oral dose is an alternative. Sexual partners must be treated simultaneously. Nitroimidazole resistance is rare but managed with high-dose metronidazole or tinidazole. Vaginal pH modifiers (lactic acid gels) and probiotic Lactobacillus preparations have supportive evidence for reducing BV recurrence but are not first-line therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole 500 mg oral twice daily for 7 days
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis. Inhibits anaerobic and protozoal DNA synthesis. Avoid alcohol during treatment and for 24 hours after; common side-effects include metallic taste and nausea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure of BV at 4 weeks 70-80%; cure of trichomoniasis 89-95% with the 7-day course (CDC 2021)

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel once daily for 5 days
medicalTreatments.description: Topical alternative for BV with equivalent efficacy to oral metronidazole and fewer systemic side-effects. Applied at bedtime; no alcohol restriction required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure 70-80% at 4 weeks in randomized trials (Schwebke & Marrazzo 2018)

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin 2% vaginal cream at bedtime for 7 days
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative to metronidazole for BV; preferred in metronidazole-intolerant patients. Caution: degrades latex condoms and diaphragms during treatment and for 72 hours after.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates equivalent to metronidazole at approximately 70-80% (Workowski & Bolan, CDC 2021)

medicalTreatments.name: Fluconazole 150 mg oral single dose
medicalTreatments.description: Convenient first-line for uncomplicated candidal vaginitis. Azole antifungal; inhibits ergosterol synthesis. Contraindicated in pregnancy. For severe VVC, give a second dose at 72 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical and mycological cure 80-90% for uncomplicated VVC caused by C. albicans (Watson et al. Cochrane 2012)

medicalTreatments.name: Clotrimazole or miconazole vaginal cream/suppositories (1-7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Over-the-counter topical azoles; appropriate for uncomplicated VVC during pregnancy or when oral fluconazole is contraindicated. Available in 1-, 3-, and 7-day formulations.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Topical azoles produce clinical cure in 80-90% of uncomplicated episodes; 7-day regimens marginally superior for more severe symptoms

medicalTreatments.name: Tinidazole 2g oral single dose
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative nitroimidazole for trichomoniasis; better tolerated than metronidazole with similar efficacy. Also active against BV. Both partners must be treated simultaneously.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate for trichomoniasis approximately 86-92% with a single 2g dose (CDC 2021)

alternativeTreatments.name: Boric acid 600 mg vaginal suppositories nightly for 14 days
alternativeTreatments.description: Evidence-supported adjunct for recurrent BV and non-albicans candidiasis (especially C. glabrata). Acidifies vaginal pH. A 2019 systematic review found cure rates of 40-100% for C. glabrata depending on concomitant treatment. Not for use in pregnancy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic Lactobacillus rhamnosus GR-1 and L. reuteri RC-14
alternativeTreatments.description: Oral probiotic combinations with the strongest evidence for reducing BV recurrence: a 2012 RCT (Martinez et al.) found 37% lower recurrence over 30 days when used after antibiotic cure. No harms; generally safe in pregnancy.

whySeeSpecialist: A gynecologist should evaluate recurrent vaginitis (three or more episodes in six months), treatment failure after two antibiotic courses, or suspected non-albicans Candida. Referral is also essential when ascending PID, pregnancy, or STI co-infection is suspected.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a gynecologist or sexual health physician who routinely performs in-office wet-mount microscopy and has access to vaginal NAAT panels. For recurrent BV or refractory candidiasis, a vulvovaginal specialist or a center with Candida culture and susceptibility testing capabilities provides the most precise care.
hospitalCriteria: Suspected pelvic inflammatory disease with systemic signs (fever, adnexal mass, or pregnancy)
Inability to tolerate oral antibiotics requiring intravenous therapy
Tubo-ovarian abscess diagnosed on pelvic ultrasound
Signs of sepsis with hemodynamic instability
Recurrent vaginitis unresponsive to all outpatient regimens requiring specialist evaluation and culture
keyFacilities: Gynecology outpatient clinics with in-office microscopy and pH testing
Sexual health and STI clinics offering same-day NAAT testing
Women's health primary care practices with vaginal culture capability
Hospital obstetric units for BV management in pregnancy
preventionStrategies: Avoid vaginal douching — it disrupts protective Lactobacillus flora and raises the risk of BV by 1.8-fold.
Use condoms consistently to reduce transmission of T. vaginalis and new BV-associated bacteria from sexual partners.
When prescribed antibiotics for any indication, ask about concurrent antifungal prophylaxis (fluconazole 150 mg once) if you have a history of antibiotic-associated candidiasis.
Maintain blood glucose control in diabetes mellitus to reduce recurrent candidal vaginitis risk.
Wear breathable, cotton-lined underwear and avoid prolonged exposure to tight, synthetic fabrics that increase perineal moisture and yeast growth.
Use unperfumed vulvar hygiene products and avoid products containing nonoxynol-9 spermicide, which disrupts the vaginal epithelial barrier.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full antibiotic or antifungal course, even if symptoms resolve early, to prevent partial treatment and relapse.
Abstain from vaginal intercourse until symptoms resolve and both partners have completed treatment in trichomoniasis.
Male partners of women with recurrent BV may benefit from concurrent penile cleansing or condom use, though evidence for male partner treatment is limited.
Change wet swimwear and gym clothing promptly; avoid prolonged perineal dampness that promotes Candida overgrowth.
Use non-irritant water-based lubricants during intercourse; avoid flavored, warming, or tingling lubricants that alter vaginal pH.
Track episode triggers in a symptom diary to identify personal precipitants (antibiotic use, hormonal changes, new partners).

recommended: Low-glycemic index diet to maintain blood glucose control and reduce candidal overgrowth risk in diabetic and pre-diabetic women
Fermented foods (plain yogurt, kefir with live cultures) providing Lactobacillus strains that may support vaginal microbiome restoration
Adequate vitamin D intake — deficiency is associated with higher BV prevalence in observational studies (Bodnar et al. 2009)
Hydration of 1.5-2 L water daily to support urinary and mucosal health
avoid: High-sugar foods and refined carbohydrates that fuel Candida overgrowth, particularly in recurrent VVC
Alcohol during metronidazole treatment (disulfiram-like reaction: nausea, flushing, tachycardia)
Excessive dairy with high sugar content; plain unsweetened yogurt is preferred over flavored varieties

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise is safe and not contraindicated during treatment. Avoid prolonged sweaty perineal exposure from tight athletic wear; change out of damp clothing promptly after exercise. Aquatic exercise in a well-chlorinated pool is acceptable; natural fresh or salt water swimming is fine and does not transmit vaginitis.
dailyManagement: Apply topical vaginal treatments at bedtime to maximize contact time and minimize leakage.
Use unscented panty liners during vaginal gel or cream courses; avoid tampons during treatment.
Track discharge, pH (at-home vaginal pH strips are available), and symptom resolution to assess treatment response.
Attend a follow-up appointment 4 weeks after BV treatment, as 30-50% of women relapse within 3 months and early retreatment reduces chronicity.
Notify sexual partners if trichomoniasis is confirmed — partner treatment on the same day is required to prevent re-infection.
prognosis: Single episodes of bacterial vaginosis respond to first-line antibiotics with cure rates of 70-80% at 4 weeks; however, 30-50% of women relapse within 3 months (Bradshaw et al. J Infect Dis 2006). Six months of weekly oral fluconazole maintenance reduces recurrent candidal vaginitis episodes by 50% compared with placebo (Sobel et al. NEJM 2004). Trichomoniasis is essentially curable with consistent partner treatment: 7-day metronidazole achieves 89-95% cure when both partners are treated simultaneously. Women with untreated BV in pregnancy face an approximate doubling of preterm birth risk. Long-term complications of untreated or recurrent vaginitis include subclinical endometritis and, in women with concurrent cervicitis, increased transmission and acquisition of HIV. With appropriate treatment and attention to recurrence triggers, the majority of women achieve sustained remission. Recurrent BV in particular is a chronic relapsing condition in some women; long-term management strategies including probiotics, boric acid, and hormonal interventions are the subject of ongoing clinical trials.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom relief typically begins within 24-72 hours of starting treatment. Full microbiological cure is confirmed at 4 weeks for BV and candidal vaginitis. Trichomoniasis is cured 5-7 days after completing the full metronidazole course. Recurrent episodes may require extended maintenance regimens over 6 months.
complications: Pelvic inflammatory disease — ascending BV-associated bacteria or trichomoniasis can infect the endometrium and fallopian tubes, causing chronic pelvic pain and infertility; screen for adnexal tenderness at every consultation.
Preterm birth and premature rupture of membranes — BV in pregnancy doubles the risk; screen and treat all symptomatic pregnant women per CDC guidelines.
Increased HIV transmission and acquisition risk — BV and trichomoniasis each raise HIV viral shedding and susceptibility roughly 1.5-2-fold due to mucosal inflammation.
Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) — affecting 5-8% of women, defined as four or more episodes per year; requires long-term azole maintenance therapy.
Cervicitis and subclinical endometritis in women with concurrent gonorrhea, chlamydia, or trichomoniasis, detected by NAAT panel and treated with targeted antibiotics.

supportResources.name: CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021 — Bacterial Vaginosis and Trichomoniasis
supportResources.description: The authoritative US treatment guideline for all causes of vaginitis, updated 2021.

supportResources.name: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 215 — Vaginitis in Nonpregnant Patients
supportResources.description: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists clinical guidance on diagnosis and management.

supportResources.name: National Vulvodynia Association
supportResources.description: Patient support and education for women with recurrent vulvar pain and vaginitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urinary Tract Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: UTI causes internal dysuria (burning inside the urethra), frequency, and urgency without abnormal vaginal discharge. Vaginitis causes external dysuria (burning at the vaginal opening when urine touches inflamed tissue) with discharge and vulvar itch. Urine culture grows bacteria in UTI; wet mount is normal. Both can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PID results from ascending infection — typically gonorrhea, chlamydia, or BV organisms — and presents with bilateral lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, and fever. Vaginitis is limited to the vaginal mucosa without uterine or adnexal involvement. The distinction determines whether oral outpatient or intravenous hospital treatment is needed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trichomoniasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted protozoal infection that causes frothy yellow-green discharge and cervical petechiae (strawberry cervix). While classified within the acute vaginitis spectrum, trichomoniasis requires partner treatment and STI contact tracing, which distinguishes its management from BV and candidal vaginitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vulvovaginal candidiasis causes thick, white, odorless discharge with intense pruritus and erythema but not the amine odor and clue cells of BV, nor the frothy discharge and strawberry cervix of trichomoniasis. Vaginal pH is normal (≤4.5) in candidiasis, distinguishing it from BV and trichomoniasis on bedside testing.

relatedConditions.name: Subacute and Chronic Vaginitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The same pathogens causing acute vaginitis can persist or recur to produce a chronic low-grade inflammatory state if inadequately treated.

relatedConditions.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Untreated or ascending acute vaginitis, particularly BV-associated organisms and trichomoniasis, is a direct precursor to PID involving the upper genital tract.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Genital herpes can co-present with or mimic vaginitis, causing vulvar ulceration, dysuria, and discharge; simultaneous NAAT testing is recommended in the STI screen.

relatedConditions.name: Amenorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Hormonal states associated with amenorrhea (low estrogen) alter vaginal pH and microbiome, predisposing to atrophic vaginitis and BV.

faqs.question: What is acute vaginitis?
faqs.answer: Acute vaginitis is sudden-onset inflammation of the vagina causing abnormal discharge, itching, burning, and odor. The three most common causes are bacterial vaginosis, vulvovaginal candidiasis, and trichomoniasis. Each has distinct discharge characteristics and requires a different antibiotic or antifungal treatment confirmed by microscopy or NAAT testing.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have bacterial vaginosis or a yeast infection?
faqs.answer: BV typically produces thin, gray-white discharge with a fishy odor and normal to mild itch. Yeast infection produces thick, white, cottage-cheese-like discharge with intense itching and no fishy smell. Vaginal pH is above 4.5 in BV and normal (≤4.5) in yeast infection. A clinician can confirm with microscopy or a vaginal pH strip.

faqs.question: Is vaginitis an STI?
faqs.answer: Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted infection requiring partner treatment. Bacterial vaginosis is associated with sexual activity but not classified as an STI. Vulvovaginal candidiasis is not sexually transmitted. All three can occur in sexually inactive women, but trichomoniasis requires sexual contact for transmission.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats acute vaginitis?
faqs.answer: BV and trichomoniasis are treated with metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 7 days. Yeast infections are treated with fluconazole 150 mg orally as a single dose or topical azole cream. The correct treatment depends on identifying the cause — using the wrong antibiotic will not resolve symptoms.

faqs.question: Can vaginitis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild bacterial vaginosis occasionally resolves without treatment as the vaginal microbiome rebalances, but most cases persist and can progress to pelvic inflammatory disease. Yeast infections rarely self-resolve without antifungal treatment. Trichomoniasis does not resolve without treatment. In pregnancy, all forms of vaginitis should be treated to reduce obstetric complications.

faqs.question: How long does vaginitis last with treatment?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically improve within 24-72 hours of starting antibiotics or antifungals. Full microbiological cure is expected by 4 weeks. BV recurs in 30-50% of women within 3 months even after successful treatment, requiring extended management strategies in those with recurrent disease.

faqs.question: Is it safe to have sex with vaginitis?
faqs.answer: Intercourse during vaginitis increases mucosal irritation and can worsen symptoms. Trichomoniasis requires abstaining until both partners have completed treatment. For BV, intercourse may alter vaginal pH and increase relapse risk. Condom use is recommended. Resume intercourse only after symptom resolution.

faqs.question: Can vaginitis cause pelvic inflammatory disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bacteria associated with BV and Trichomonas vaginalis can ascend through the cervix to infect the endometrium and fallopian tubes, causing PID. Signs of ascending infection include lower abdominal pain, fever, cervical motion tenderness, and adnexal tenderness on examination. Prompt treatment of vaginitis reduces this risk.

faqs.question: Can vaginitis affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy doubles the risk of preterm birth and premature rupture of membranes. Trichomoniasis is also associated with preterm delivery. All pregnant women with symptoms of vaginitis should be tested and treated. Metronidazole is safe in pregnancy from the second trimester onward.

faqs.question: What causes recurrent yeast infections?
faqs.answer: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (four or more episodes per year) affects 5-8% of women. Triggers include uncontrolled diabetes, prolonged antibiotic use, immunosuppression, oral contraceptives, and non-albicans Candida species resistant to standard azoles. Weekly fluconazole maintenance for 6 months reduces recurrence by roughly 50%.

faqs.question: What does vaginitis discharge look like?
faqs.answer: BV discharge is thin, gray-white, and homogeneous with a fishy odor. Yeast infection discharge is thick, white, and clumpy (cottage-cheese texture) without odor. Trichomoniasis discharge is frothy, yellow-green, and copious. Normal vaginal discharge is clear to white, odorless, and varies in amount across the menstrual cycle.

faqs.question: Do I need to see a doctor for vaginitis?
faqs.answer: Over-the-counter antifungals are appropriate only for previously diagnosed yeast infection with typical symptoms. First-episode vaginal symptoms, symptoms with odor, symptoms in pregnancy, or treatment failure after one course all require clinical evaluation — up to 50% of self-diagnosed yeast infections are misidentified and actually BV or trichomoniasis.

faqs.question: Can vaginitis cause bleeding?
faqs.answer: Light spotting can occur from inflamed, friable vaginal or cervical mucosa in trichomoniasis (strawberry cervix pattern) or severe BV-associated endometritis. Heavy bleeding is not a feature of vaginitis and warrants further investigation for cervical pathology, ectopic pregnancy, or uterine causes.

faqs.question: What is the difference between vaginitis and cervicitis?
faqs.answer: Vaginitis affects the vaginal mucosa; cervicitis affects the cervix, typically caused by gonorrhea or chlamydia. Both cause discharge, but cervicitis produces purulent endocervical discharge visible at the os and is associated with STI contact tracing. NAAT testing distinguishes the two and guides targeted treatment.

faqs.question: Can men get vaginitis?
faqs.answer: Men cannot get vaginitis (which is specific to the vaginal anatomy) but can carry and transmit Trichomonas vaginalis, causing urethritis in some. Male partners of women with trichomoniasis require simultaneous treatment with metronidazole or tinidazole. Men generally do not require treatment for their female partners' BV or yeast infections.

metaTitle: Acute Vaginitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Acute vaginitis: sudden vaginal inflammation from bacteria, yeast, or parasites. Symptoms, diagnosis, antibiotic and antifungal treatment reviewed here.
